comment_content: I need to know, if you get dragged out your bed by your feet, what do you automatically do with your hands?  Do most people throw their hands back like they are going down a water slide or rollercoaster? I feel like I would try to stop this, not set back to watch or enjoy.
reply_content: This only my experience, I cannot speak for anyone else.  And I only share because this is included in the Netflix show.  At first, there was shock/surprise and my arms 'startled' up, then I tried to grab hold of something to stop the momentum.  But I was sleeping in a pile of sheets on the floor of the room next to mine, and neither they nor the pillow provided me an anchor on which to fasten myself.  I began to scream for help, one of the people in the room turned on the lights, and the motion stopped.  Being in a haunting is difficult, primarily, because you begin to question the lens by which you see the world.  In actuality, I was laying face down, but it looks like the producers felt that a face up scene was more powerful?  Slight variation from the moment itself, but they did effectively capture the fell. I hope that you enjoy the show, and please be well.

reply_content: Being the one that endured that moment - I can share with you that it did leave me with PTSD.  After that day, March 10th, 1985, I would instead walk across the entire campus in order to use the group showers in the sports locker room. Sadly, I think that many people quietly suffer from life's challenges whether it be personal loss, poverty, addiction, etc. and finding ways to function beyond that pain is an important goal.  Stay safe.  Spoiler alert: I live. :)

comment_content: True Haunting: Eerie Hall features my husband and creative partner Jeff & our great friend Chris who went through the haunting.  It is gratifying to see their meaningful story shared by James Wan, Mark Lewis, and the whole team.
reply_content: Hi M!  Even after 4 1/2 years of preparing for this moment, it is still mind-boggling.  Even though there is a clear and linear connection between the events themselves and this Netflix production forty years later, the hundreds of dots in between them - sometimes unnoticed, and sometimes celebrated (like the "Please Talk with Me" film project that you directed) - often felt as though they happened by pure chance.  And yet, we know, as Jeff pointed out, it always felt as though this story - somehow - wanted to tell itself.  Here is hoping that sharing, on so great a scale, will be helpful to people who are still searching for their answers.
